description: 폐쇄된 내부 형상을 파내는 가공. 칩 배출이 어려움.

tool_selection.subtype: Square 또는 Radius (코너R 있으면 코너 강도 증가)
tool_selection.length: 짧은 날장(LOC) 권장. 깊은 포켓은 넥 달린 공구

strategy: 헬리컬 진입 (plunge 금지) — 공구 손상 방지
코너 감속 — 코너 R 근처 이송 70% 감속
common_mistakes: 중심 절삭 불가 공구로 직진 진입 → 공구 파손
깊은 포켓에 긴 공구 사용 → 떨림 → 넥 타입 사용

description: 워크피스 외벽을 깎는 가공. 가장 일반적.

tool_selection.subtype: Square (직각 벽면), Radius (코너 R 있는 벽면)

description: 공구 직경 = 홈 폭, 양면 동시 절삭.
원호 궤적으로 ae를 작게 유지하며 ap를 최대로.

recommended_strategy: Enter closed pockets with helical (circular) ramp interpolation using a helix diameter >110-120% of cutter diameter. Apply HEM trochoidal paths for roughing to maximize tool life.
common_pitfalls: Straight plunge with non-center-cutting tool → breakage
Long-reach tool on shallow pocket → chatter and poor finish
Ignoring corner feed reduction → corner burn-out
source_name: Harvey Performance — Most Common Methods of Tool Entry

recommended_tool_subtype: square or corner-radius endmill, 4-5 flute, 35-45° helix
recommended_strategy: Use climb (down) milling with moderate ae for roughing and very light ae for finishing to achieve wall straightness and Ra targets.
common_pitfalls: Conventional (up) milling on soft materials → built-up edge
Excessive ap on slender tool → wall taper
Skipping climb direction → poor surface finish
source_name: Harvey Performance — Diving Into the Depth of Cut

recommended_strategy: With full diameter engagement (ae=100%D), reduce feed and axial DOC and use through-coolant to evacuate chips; prefer trochoidal slotting when geometry allows.
common_pitfalls: 4+ flute tool in a full slot → chip packing and breakage
Insufficient coolant pressure → chip re-cutting
Full fz of side milling applied → overload

recommended_tool_subtype: ball-nose endmill, 4-flute variable pitch, tapered neck
recommended_strategy: Select stepover from target cusp/scallop height; keep stepover above ~12.5%D to avoid diminishing returns and compensate Vc for reduced effective diameter at shallow Z engagement.
common_pitfalls: Using nominal Vc at tip → severe rubbing and tip wear
Stepover too small → machining time with no finish gain
Long tool without tapered neck → chatter on walls

recommended_strategy: Match cutter diameter to 1.3-1.5× workpiece width, enter with rolling arc, and choose insert geometry by ap/fz window; use sharp PVD inserts at ap 0.5-0.8 mm for finish.
common_pitfalls: Cutter centered on workpiece → unfavorable entry angle, insert chipping
Thin walls with high fz → deflection and poor flatness
Wrong insert geometry for ap/fz combination

recommended_strategy: Prefer helical interpolation for closed forms; use 1-3° linear ramp for open entries and keep helix diameter >110-120% of the cutter diameter.

key_parameters.Vc_m_min: same as slotting for the material
key_parameters.ap_pct_D: per pass driven by ramp angle

common_pitfalls: Non-center-cutting tool on plunge → tip failure
Ramp angle too steep in hard steel → chipping
Helix diameter <110%D → bottom gets skipped

recommended_tool_subtype: single-profile or multi-profile thread mill (solid carbide or indexable)
recommended_strategy: Helically interpolate to produce the thread; arc-in / arc-out entry avoids witness marks. One tool handles multiple diameters of the same pitch; superior to tapping in hard or blind-hole work.

key_parameters.fz_mm: adjusted for effective diameter at thread radius
key_parameters.ap_pct_D: thread depth in one helical pass

common_pitfalls: Straight radial plunge into full depth → witness mark on thread
Not compensating fz for the cutter-vs-hole radius geometry
Ignoring direction (climb) for internal vs. external threads

recommended_tool_subtype: chamfer mill (60/82/90/100/120°) or double-cut deburring chamfer cutter
recommended_strategy: Calculate speed and feed at the effective cutting diameter (not nominal tip), use climb direction and light axial DOC; double-cut deburring tools support higher feeds.
common_pitfalls: Using nominal diameter for RPM → severe tip rub
Conventional milling → ragged chamfer edge
Chamfer cutter used as spot drill in hard material → chipping
source_name: Harvey Performance — Multiple Uses of a Chamfer Mill

recommended_tool_subtype: center-cutting plunge endmill or dedicated insert plunge cutter
recommended_strategy: Drive cutting force into the spindle axis for maximum rigidity in long-overhang or chatter-prone setups; index ~75%D between plunges and finish walls with a side pass.
common_pitfalls: Non-center-cutting tool → tip failure on plunge
Step too large → ledges and uncut material
Skipping finishing side pass → poor wall finish
source_name: Harvey Performance — Plunge & Pocket Milling in 4140

recommended_strategy: Select point angle by material, pre-spot when needed, use through-coolant for L/D>3 and peck drilling for L/D>5 or gummy materials.
common_pitfalls: Walking on uneven surface without spotting
No through-coolant on deep hole → chip packing
Wrong point angle for work material

recommended_strategy: Leave 0.1-0.3 mm stock on diameter, run at ~2/3 of the drilling Vc and 2-3× the drilling feed per revolution; use rigid or floating holder and flood/through-coolant.

key_parameters.Vc_m_min: ~2/3 of drilling Vc for same material
key_parameters.fz_mm: 2-3× drilling fn (per rev, not per tooth)

common_pitfalls: Using drilling speed on the reamer → bell-mouth and oversize
Too little stock → burnishing, poor finish
Misalignment → taper and chatter marks

recommended_tool_subtype: spiral-point (through) or spiral-flute (blind) cutting tap, or forming tap
recommended_strategy: Use synchronized rigid tapping or tension-compression holder; cutting oil mandatory; Vc 5-15 m/min in steel for cutting taps, higher for forming taps on ductile materials.
common_pitfalls: Wrong pre-drill diameter (cutting vs. forming tap)
Insufficient lubrication → tap welding and breakage
Spiral-point tap in blind hole → chip packing
